latest.title: Transaction in Own Shares
latest.content:
28 Apr 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 0964C
McBride PLC
28 April 2026
28 April 2026
McBride plc
Transaction in Own Shares
McBride plc (the "Company") announces today it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares of 10 pence each ("Ordinary Shares") through Peel Hunt LLP (the "Broker").
Ordinary Shares
Date of purchase
27 April 2026
Number of Ordinary Shares purchased:
5,711
Lowest price per Ordinary Share (pence):
159.00
Highest price per Ordinary Share (pence):
160.00
Volume weighted average price per day (pence):
159.56
The Company will purchase the above Ordinary Shares through the Broker on the London Stock Exchange (the "Purchases") and will subsequently cancel them.
Such Purchases form part of the Company's existing buyback programme and were effected pursuant to the non-discretionary agreement entered into with the Broker, as announced on 1st December 2025.
Following the Purchases and cancellation of these Ordinary Shares, the Company will have 175,365,629 Ordinary Shares in issue (excluding treasury shares), hold 42,041 Ordinary Shares in treasury, and have 594,610,458 non-cumulative redeemable preference shares of 0.1 pence each in the Company ("B Shares") in issue. B Shares carry no rights to attend, speak or vote at Company meetings, except on a resolution relating to the winding up of the Company.
Once the Ordinary Shares are cancelled, the total number of voting rights in the Company will be 175,365,629. This figure may be used by shareholders of the Company as the denominator for the calculations by which they can determine if they are required to notify their interest, or a change to their interest, in the Company pursuant to the Disclosure and Transparency Rules (the "DTRs").
The Company confirms that, in accordance with DTR 5.5.1R, following the Purchases the percentage of voting rights attributable to the Ordinary Shares held in treasury is 0.02 per cent.
The tables below contain detailed information about the Purchases.

company_30s.what_they_do: McBride plc designs, manufactures and supplies private‑label cleaning and hygiene products for both households and professional customers.  Think dish‑soap, laundry detergent, floor cleaners and related consumables that are sold under other brands.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The household cleaning market is a $120 billion global industry that is steadily growing as consumers shift to more brand‑agnostic, value‑oriented goods.  McBride’s contract‑manufacturing model lets it serve a broad roster of brands, giving it a diversified revenue base and a foothold in a sector that is resistant to economic cycles.
company_30s.current_state: The company is in a consolidation phase.  After a recent interim results period and a series of director changes, it appears to be tightening its focus on core private‑label contracts while exploring potential acquisitions.  The stock sits at the very low end of its recent trading range, suggesting a steep trough that could be primed for a rebound.

rns_narrative: McBride has been quietly restructuring: a director appointment in March, a 6‑month interim earnings report in February, and a proposed acquisition announced in April.  These moves signal a shift from a fragmented portfolio to a more streamlined, high‑margin private‑label operation.
The company is also taking steps to improve liquidity – a transaction in own shares and a trading‑conditions update – which could provide the cash needed to fund growth initiatives or pay down debt.
The narrative is one of a firm pivoting from a defensive stance to a more aggressive growth strategy, with a focus on capturing higher‑margin contract work and exploring new product lines.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet optimism – the community is largely silent, but the few bullish voices highlight McBride’s potential to rebound after a deep sell‑off.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bulls point to the company’s strong private‑label base and the recent acquisition proposal as catalysts for a sharp upside.  They argue that the stock’s low valuation relative to its peers creates a clear upside if the business stabilises.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears worry about the company’s recent volatility, the fact that it has yet to hit a clear support level, and the risk that the proposed acquisition could fail to materialise.  They also flag the lack of a clear, short‑term earnings beat.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: Sentiment has been flat for months, but the latest RNS items have sparked a subtle uptick in discussion, suggesting that the market may soon reassess the company’s fundamentals.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat – no recent spikes in search interest, which is a classic sign that the market has not yet priced in any catalyst.

near_term_catalysts: A clear earnings beat in the next quarterly report, especially if it shows higher contract volumes and margin expansion.
Completion of the proposed acquisition or a positive update on its progress, which would signal a new revenue stream.
A sustained uptick in trading volume that breaks the 20‑day moving average, indicating that institutional buyers are stepping in.
Any announcement of a new product line or partnership with a major consumer‑goods brand.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a 5‑minute volume spike that exceeds 200% of the 20‑day average and a price break above the 20‑day moving average.  Also monitor the 52‑week range; a bounce past the mid‑point is a strong confirmation.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock appears to be in the early stages of a crash‑recovery cycle – it has just hit a new trough and the APEX score suggests a tight compression.  A mean‑reversion play will need a clear catalyst before the price starts to climb.  If the next earnings release shows upside, the window for entry will open within the next 2–3 weeks.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If the stock recovers to the midpoint of its 52‑week range, the upside is roughly 200% from today’s price.  The deep crash (over 70% from the peak) means the risk‑reward ratio is highly skewed in favour of the trader – a small move up can wipe out the entire loss from the plunge.  The signal colour hierarchy would rank this as a GREEN or YELLOW bottom, which historically has a ~40% win rate but offers large rebounds.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The main downside is that the company has not yet proved it can sustain higher volumes or margins.  If the acquisition stalls or the earnings miss, the stock could slide further into the lower 10‑percentile of its range, potentially eroding the upside.  A worst‑case scenario would be a drop to 30% of the pre‑crash high, leaving a 70% loss.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Risk: 70% down from the peak (roughly 1:1 loss potential if the price stays flat).  Reward: 200% upside if the price reaches the 52‑week midpoint.  The asymmetry (1 : 2.5) makes this a classic contrarian play.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter on a pullback that touches the 20‑day moving average and confirm with a 5‑minute volume spike.  Size the position so that a 5% drop from today’s price would still leave you with a 15% unrealised loss – a comfortable cushion.  Set a stop just below the 20‑day average to protect against a false breakout.
playbook.if_watching: Hold cash until the next earnings release.  If the company reports higher contract volumes and a margin expansion, place a small position with a stop 5% below the earnings‑release support level.  If the price also breaks above the 20‑day average, add to the position.
playbook.if_passing: If the company announces a failure to complete the acquisition or a significant downgrade by a major rating agency, consider closing the position to avoid a deeper plunge.

ai_insights.bottom_line: McBride plc sits at a deep trough, but a clear earnings beat or acquisition completion could trigger a sharp rebound, offering a highly asymmetric upside that outweighs the modest downside risk.
